title: Dropped Stitch Troubleshooter
hero: Find out what's causing your dropped stitches - and get one clear fix you can try right now.
description:
Dropped stitches happen to every knitter — but they don't have to derail your project.
This interactive Troubleshooter walks you through a short diagnostic to understand why it's happening and what you can do about it.

You'll choose the option that best matches what you're seeing, and the guide will give you:

  • the most likely cause

  • a simple, practical fix

  • one action step you can take right away

Clear, calm, and to the point — so you can get back to knitting with confidence.
excerpt: Find the best fix for your dropped stitch problem.
imageAlt: picking up a dropped stitch in machine knitting
metaTitle: Dropped Stitch Troubleshooter - Knit by Machine
metaDescription: Find out what's causing your dropped stitches with this quick troubleshooting tool. Get practical fixes for edge tension, transfer technique, needle problems, and more.

schemaMarkup.name: Dropped Stitch Troubleshooter

step.name: Identify where the stitch drops
step.text: Choose when and where you usually see the dropped stitch to narrow down the most likely cause.

step.name: Review the likely cause
step.text: Based on your selection, the tool reveals the most probable reason for the dropped stitch.

step.name: Take the recommended action
step.text: Follow a quick, practical fix tailored to your situation, with one clear step to try immediately.

schemaMarkup.description: A quick diagnostic tool that helps machine knitters identify why they're dropping stitches and provides a clear, practical fix.

wizardFlow.questionTitle: When do you usually notice the dropped stitch?
wizardFlow.questionSubtitle: Choose the option that sounds most like what you're seeing.

options.label: On the edge of my knitting
On the same needle over and over
Randomly in the middle of the fabric
I'm not sure – it feels random

results.causeTitle: Edge tension or patterning at the edge
results.explanation: The edge stitches may be too loose, losing contact with the gate pegs, or patterning may be pulling more on the edge needles.
tryThisItems: Add a claw weight or extra weight near the edge.
Check that your edge needles are fully in working position.
If you're patterning, try a plain-knit edge (1–2 stitches) before the pattern starts.
results.encouragement: Edge stitches are fussy for everyone. A little extra weight and attention usually fixes it.

results.explanation: Stitches may be falling off while they're on the tool or while you're moving them to new needles.
tryThisItems: Slow down and keep the stitches fully on the prongs before you move them.
Keep the tool level with the needle bed instead of tipping it.
Practice on scrap yarn until the motions feel smooth.
results.encouragement: This is pure practice. The more you transfer on scrap, the more automatic it feels.

results.causeTitle: A problem needle (bent, sticky, or damaged)
results.explanation: One stubborn needle can cause repeated dropped stitches in the same column.
tryThisItems: Mark the problem needle with a bit of yarn.
Remove and inspect it for burrs, bends, or rough spots.
Replace it if in doubt – it's not worth fighting.
results.encouragement: If one needle keeps misbehaving, retiring it is an easy win.

results.explanation: The yarn may be feeding too loosely or too tightly, or the carriage may not be moving smoothly.
tryThisItems: Knit a small swatch and adjust your tension dial up or down one number.
Check that the mast, tension dial, and yarn path are smooth with no snags.
Make sure you're moving the carriage all the way across with even speed.
results.encouragement: Small tension tweaks can make a big difference. Keep notes as you experiment.

results.causeTitle: A mix of tension and technique
results.explanation: When things feel random, it usually means a few small factors are stacking up.
tryThisItems: Knit a 20–30 row test swatch and watch where the fabric misbehaves.
Check edge weights, overall tension, and any tools you use often.
Note when the drop happens (edge, transfer, middle) – then rerun this checker.
results.encouragement: You're not doing anything 'wrong.' You're just gathering clues. Each small test gives you more control.
